title_tesim: Actress Olive Young at the Otis Art Institute or the Elks Lodge No. 99, Los Angeles, 1928
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2357 P1-6.  Pacific [illegible].  1928. Pls ret negs.  IN THE GARDEN OF THE WESTERN GODS.  Olive Young, right, "Chinese Mary pickford" popular motion picture player of Chinese and oriental roles, Rosemary Chew, noted Chinese soprano and artist's model, photographed in the garden of the Otis Art Institute in Los Angeles with the new Elk's Club as a back ground.  Showing frieze of heroes etc. on wall - thus, in a garden of the Western gods.

description_tesim: Olive Young was an actress who was a major star at the Great Wall studio in Shanghai from 1926 to 1929. Born Olive Young in St. Joseph, Missouri,  she first visited China at the age of 16, when her parents sent her to Hong Kong to complete her education. Two years later she began her acting career in Shanghai. After 1929, she returned to the United States and had a brief acting career in Hollywood.
Olive Young seated in front of wood paneling, in damask dress

combined_subject_ssim: Elks (Fraternal order) Lodge No. 99 (Los Angeles, Calif.)--Buildings

title_tesim: Santa Monica College student with wreath and article about poet Ina Donna Coolbrith, Santa Monica, 1934
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Bartlett, Santa Monica Jr. College Student, 1934, Shaw, Ina Coalbrith [sic] [should read: Coolbrith], Poetess Laureate of California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The 1888 horse-shoe-shaped shell art wreath made by Ina Donna Coolbrith. The wreath was composed of sea shells interwoven with moss gathered at Santa Monica. The flowers represented include Mariposa lily, Shasta daisy, Copa de Oro, camelia, rose and other flowers. The wreath was a gift to Elizabeth Dalton Perry and William Hayes Perry. At the time of this photograph the wreath belonged to the Perrys' nephew John H. Crum of Santa Monica. The shell wreath is an example of Victorian era domestic crafts.
Young woman seated at small desk, holding book, with wreath mounted in frame and article, propped on desk, about Ina Donna Coolbrith, titled Sappho of the Western Sea
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

title_tesim: Negative envelope describing photograph of Thelma Peairs interest in photography, 1930
caption_tesim: Text reads: 2510, Venice girls, 1930.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  Silhouettes.  Miss Thelma Peairs, "Miss Venice-of-America," California, now has a hobby, that of news photography in which art she "shoots" visiting celebreties [sic].  Feb. 18 she snapped Calvin and Mrs. Coolidge on their arrival in Los Angeles, their first visit to the orange and moving picture capital.  Miss Peairs has been Miss Venice for the past three years.

summary_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: Tia Juana.  Bartlett.  Outside the "longest bar in the world" at Tia Juana, which is thronged with Eastern tenderfeet during the summer overland vacation season, hovers the native vendor of tourist goods, and he speaks good "Engleese;" will if you desire, escort you through various back doors of the little town, especially when he sees two husky Mexican policemen, each wearing a brace of guns, extra ammunition, handcuffs, blackjacks and other implements of war, in their motor truck, which is a travell-jail, leave for manhunt or something in the outskirts of town.  Miss Jean Myras, Hollywood model, trying her best not to look "professional" in picture.  1931

title_tesim: Vacationers on sunny terrace overlooking the coast of Southern California and fleet of ships, Long Beach 1933
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: Long Beach, Calif.; Views from Chamber of Commerce; 1932, 1940; city parks, skyscrapers, beach girls; p-1-2-3-4-5; No. 2562
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Text from a negative sleeve linked to a similar image appears to describe this photo as well: Long Beach, fleet from roof garden. Compo..., All Year Club, Red Island RR. 1933. [Duplicate image ark no. 21198/zz002bd3jb]

Probably photographed at the Breakers Hotel (210 E. Ocean Boulevard), that stood adjacent to the Capitol/Tracy Theatre (demolished 1974), across from the Long Beach Municipal Auditorium (now the Long Beach Convention and Entertainment Center).
This image was used in an advertisement for the All Year Club of Southern California, a Los Angeles based travel agency that promoted low cost vacations in Southern California.

Photograph of two women seated at a table on a terrace, with a girl standing apart from them at the terrace wall. A sign for the Capitol Theatre is viewed in the immediate background at left. At center ground, the Long Beach Municipal Auditorium (demolished in 1975) with a fleet of six ships in the ocean beyond.
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

title_tesim: Date palm with harvesting platform, Indio, 1931-1948
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2181 P1 - 2 Indio - Calif.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  DATE PALM HAREM.  Indio, Calif., oasis of date palms, showing at right male trees alongside rows of female, bearing, trees.  On the female trees are great bunches of ripe dates which are gathered and tended from platforms like one in second view enclosed.  It is 16 ft. up to the fruit.  Some of these grees [sic] bear 250 lbs. of dates.  Oct. is ripening season.  DEGLET NOOR variety.  Few us[e?] about 10. title_tesim: Japanese style flower arrangement with rose in vase by Margaret Preininger, Los Angeles, 1935
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Miss Preininger, Flowers, Los Angeles Junior College, 1935, 2595 P 1 to 21
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Single rose in footed glass vase. Margaret Preininger published a book titled Japanese flower arrangement for modern homes on December 1, 1935. At that time she was the chairman of Cultural Arts at  Los Angeles Junior College. According to the review in Kirkus Reviews, the 35 pages of text explain the four important Japanese Schools and the adaptations of their principles to American flowers and settings; the book includes 52 large reproductions of arrangements and 100 line drawings showing method of procedure; and chapters on vases, holders, flower selection, color, seasons, background and table settings
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

description_tesim: Text reads: View in observatory which houses the 60-inch telescope at the Carnegie Institute's observatory on the summit of Mt. Wilson in Southern California showing Friday night party of tourists taking their turns at looking through the scope at Jupiter, 130,000,000 miles away.  Visibility is generally fine on Mt. Wilson.  In addition to the 60-inch telescope shown here the obser vatory is equipped with the 100-inch telescope.  Now that the Institute is planning to create a 200-inch telescope, which is to be the world's largest, it is expected that this instrument will also be located on the summit of Mt. Wilson, altho this location has not been definitely settled.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

title_tesim: Dane Gorrell with 1888 horseshoe-shaped shell art wreath made by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith, Santa Monica, 1953
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2954 File B Ina Coolbrith Poetry Laureate California 1915-1928 John Crum Collection Miss Dane Gorrell of Marin County model with plaque year 1953

Text from newspaper caption: Shell Painting: Dane Gorrell with Poetess Ina Coolbrith's horseshoe design of California flowers modeled from sea shells interwoven with moss gathered at Santa Monica 65 years ago. Memento was treasure of late W. H. Perry family ["Heirloom Plaque Shows Poetess' Art," Los Angeles Times, October 12, 1953]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Dane Gorrell, with 1888 horse-shoe-shaped shell art wreath made by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith. The flowers represented include Mariposa lily, Shasta daisy, Copa de Oro, camelia, rose and other flowers. The wreath was a gift to Elizabeth Dalton Perry and William Hayes Perry. At the time of this photograph the wreath belonged to the Perrys' nephew John H. Crum of Santa Monica. The shell wreath is an example of Victorian era domestic crafts.
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

title_tesim: Paintings in an American naive style by Jane Wooster Scott at the Windemere Hotel, Santa Monica, 1954
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: John Lindsay...Lady Windemere. 8/5/54.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The Hotel Windemere was located at 1431 Ocean Ave. Built in 1909, it was demolished in 1962. John Lindsay, a southern California architect, owned the building from at least 1954 until its demolition. According to newspaper reports from 1954 to 1956, he and Jane Wooster Scott were a couple.

Photograph of 2 paintings in an American naïve style of a landscape with sled ina snowscape (left) and a pond with swimmers and fishermen. Signed "Wooster," the paintings are early works by Jane Wooster Scott (b. 1929). The paintings hang on a wall covered in striped wall paper at the Windemere Hotel.

title_tesim: Letter from June (Moss?) regarding photo orders for "Rigoletto," Santa Monica, 1956
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 15-30, Rigoletto, #30 Ludwig-Porta, April 6/7/56, S.M. OPERA, Porta - Ludwig, Vivaldi - Haley, cruise, Brut

Letter text: Bart--One more order for: Rigoletto Photos. Vittorio wants 2 of No. 26, and 2 of the pose with Nelli pat which seems to be numbered on the pasteup. He would only pay $2.25, the rat! Unless Nellipat wants some, I believe that's all the orders for Rigoletto. Mrs. Ritt is showing the pictures once more on Monday, June 25th, and will be finished with them and will get them back to you. June

She wears a dress and bonnet made of a light netted material. The dress appears to have hoopskirts, and is trimmed with flower appliqués. The bonnet ties under her chin with a ribbon. The photograph was taken at the home of Adelbert Bartlett, located at 535 15th St., Santa Monica.

title_tesim: Pearl Jennings from "O.K. By Me" directed by Louise Glaum, Los Angeles, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Louise Glaum, 1952, O.K. by Me, Play, Louise Glaum.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Louise Glaum was the director of this production of "O.K. By Me," a play by Sheldon Shepard. Mrs. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director, who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first "vamp" actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.

This image is of cast member Pearl Jennings who played the role of Belle, the maid, in the production of the Sheldon Sheppard play "O.K. By Me." Jennings is wearing a dark dress, necklace, earrings, and bracelet.
Photograph of a re-touched photograph
This production was referenced by two Los Angeles Times articles, "Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater." 11 Oct. 1952, and "Beaux Arts Will Reopen." 19 Sep. 1952
This image appears to be rephotographed and then drawn on, made evident by the lines (possibly ink) below her eyes and along the frame of her face by her ears.
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

title_tesim: Indoor swimming pool at the Sorrento Beach Club, Santa Monica, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Mr. Philbin, Sorrento Beach Club 1952, S.M. Pool.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: P. H. Philbin, of Chicago, a former owner of the Sorrento Beach Club building, had planned a 12-story apartment building on the site in 1953. The project included utilizing the massive concrete Sorrento Beach Club structure which had been abandoned in an unfinished state. The project was controversial, as some property owners felt that it would mar the beauty of the Santa Monica Palisades Park.
This image is of the drained indoor swimming pool on the third floor of the Sorrento Club.
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

title_tesim: Page of Article "A Californian in the Holy Land", circa 1929
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1929 Beirut to Jerusalem, Bartlett copies pages, Beirut-Jerusalem.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This page contains information on Bartlett's arrival in Syria, where he is confronted with the use of American automobiles and other Western infrastructure, such as paved roads. This page has an image of an American automobile with Ancient Roman aqueducts in the background. There is also the image of two children walking hand in hand to school along the road Christ walked to Calvary.

This image is a copy of a page of an article, "A Californian in the Holy Land", written by Adelbert Bartlett in 1929. This article covers his trip through Beirut, Jerusalem, and Palestine. He traveled with a professor, who is not named. Various locals guided them on their trip, as documented in article. Once arriving in Beirut via ship, they traveled primarily by car.

title_tesim: Photograph of cacti and bell with caption beginning "Christmas Church Bells"
caption_tesim: Christmas Bells in California are apt to be silver church bells--except in the mountains where the traditional sleigh bells still ring. And Christmas roses are the ethereal paper-like blossoms of the cacti that grow in old gardens such as this one at the ancient Mission of San Miguel. "White flowers of love its walls shall climb, Soft bells of peace shall ring its chime, Its days shall all be holy time."--Whittier

caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2761 P43

description_tesim: The Oasis Hotel, designed by Lloyd Wright (eldest son of Frank Lloyd Wright), constructed 1923-25, located at what is today the southwest corner of Palm Canyon Drive and Tahquitz Canyon Way.

A waiter serves guests in the courtyard. Three men and one woman are visible in the photo. They sit on the lawn in directors chairs. All wear hats, the men in ivy-style caps. Two of the men wear suit jackets; one wears a button down shirt and tie. They are surrounded by young trees and lawn furniture.  The hotel sits in the background on the left-hand side. A second structure sits on the opposite side of a picket fence. A truck or van can be seen beyond the fence. Photographed at the Oasis Hotel, Palm Springs.

title_tesim: Pages of article, “They Give the Kids a Chance,” circa 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SE Post Story. 1952. Post Story. Ntl Music Legue [sic]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This image is a copy of two pages from the article “They Give the Kids a Chance,” by Morton B. Hunt, regarding the National Music League, Inc. The right-hand page recounts the story of Angeline Collins, a young singer who was not able to sign up with a large management agency because of her inexperience.  Collins’ story continues on the second page. The story demonstrates how the league assisted the unknown musician with getting her first bookings. Photo at top: Pianist Lilian Kallir performing for an audience; bottom left: Anna “Mama” Molyneaux; top right: League musicians Karl Brock and Sylvia Rosenberg outside Carnegie Hall; bottom right: Opera star Ezio Pinza with Lilian Kallir and Helen Clayton.

The National Music League began in 1924 as a not-for-profit bureau concerned with booking amateur classical musicians for concert work. The league was based in New York City.   Article citation: Hunt, Morton B. "They Give the Kids a Chance." The Saturday Evening Post, May 10, 1952.
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

title_tesim: Typescript document with article page, “They Give the Kids a Chance,” circa 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SE Post Story. 1952. Post Story. Ntl Music Legue [sic]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This image is a copy of a page from the article “They Give the Kids a Chance,” by Morton B. Hunt, alongside a typescript manuscript with corrections regarding the National Music League, Inc. On the left, the article page recounts work-related stories from two young female musicians – Frances Magnes and Lilian Kallir. On the right, the typescript document (likely written before 1952), promotes the league and solicits applications from qualified musicians. In the document, the League is described as a non-profit bureau interested in booking amateur classical musicians under 30 for concert work.

The National Music League began in 1924 as a not-for-profit bureau concerned with booking amateur classical musicians for concert work. The league was based in New York City.   Article citation: Hunt, Morton B. "They Give the Kids a Chance." The Saturday Evening Post, May 10, 1952.

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

description_tesim: This image is a copy of two pages from the article “They Give the Kids a Chance,” by Morton B. Hunt, regarding the National Music League, Inc. The pages describe how the league sets musicians up with concert tours, how their musicians are compensated, and the benefits of belonging with the league. To this, pianist Jim Wolfe describes his working experiences in Brazil. Also, the league’s number of concert bookings and gross income is compared to other talent agencies of the day – namely Columbia Artists Management and N.C.A.C.  Anna “Mama” Molyneaux’s role and history in the organization is also discussed.

The National Music League began in 1924 as a not-for-profit bureau concerned with booking amateur classical musicians for concert work. The league was based in New York City.   Article citation: Hunt, Morton B. "They Give the Kids a Chance." The Saturday Evening Post, May 10, 1952.

subject_tesim: Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)
description_tesim: Photograph view facing north toward the Santa Monica Bay and lighthouse building on Will Rogers State Beach, taken atop a Palisades cliff. A portion of Pacific Coast Highway is seen bottom left; a cactus grows with shrubbery on the cliffs at right.
The lighthouse building was purchased by Will Rogers in 1928 and eventually demolished in 1978. It was not a functioning lighthouse. Co-ordinates derived from original lighthouse location.

This image appears to pre-date a photograph of Caroline Bartlett taken on this spot in 1946. The image of Bartlett shows an additional structure (possibly a lifeguard tower) on the landing next to the lighthouse. The lighthouse tower has also gained an additional sign advertising "Seafood" next to "Swimsuits." (See image ark no. 21198/zz002d86c7)

description_tesim: At some point after WWII the rustic branch fence at Palisades Park was replaced with two types: a pre-cast concrete fence with a criss-cross design and a wood board fence. Bartlett photographs of Palisades Park dated 1946 show the rustic branch fence and the wood rail fence.

Photograph of a woman on the Palisades Park pathway. The woman, wearing a 1940s era dress and hair style, is leaning against the park’s original rustic eucalyptus branch fence. She is shaded by a large eucalyptus tree. The view faces south, following the cliffs toward Pacific Coast Highway, Santa Monica Beach and Pier. The Sorrento club can be seen buttressed against the cliffs along the highway. Across the way, beach homes, clubs and businesses follow the coastline.

ark:/21198/zz002d550q (print)
description_tesim: Text reads: Adelbert Bartlett, 1572 W Pico Blvd, Los Angeles, Calif.  Exclusive.  Note: Try the roto section service which is supplied to many colleges for inserts in college newspaper, also health magazines.  Picture of girls in a boat: Members of the Row-A-Way Club, recently organized aquatic sports group, at Santa Monica (Calif.) Junior College, take to one of the Los Angeles beach city's new life-guard patrol skiffs for a pretty picture intended as showing "form" at the oars in preparation for mid-winter practice in rowing and sailing in the new Santa Monica yacht harbor, nearest popular Pacific beach to Hollywood and Los Angeles.  Virginia Constable is at the tiller-oar.  Gym groups work outdoors practically every day all winter long in Sou. Calif., which is said to account for at least some of the pep these young women exhibit (adv.).  Left to right the girls are: Virginia Putnam, Nedra Herz, Isobel Millier, Mary McKay, Bettie Lou Slaughter, Margaret Barnhisel, Betty Christy, Flora Mae Prescott, Viola Bunker, Virginia Constable, a number of whom are members of socially prominent families in Southern California.  ##  Picture of girls lined up with oars: Same group of girls setting up and other maneuvers preparatory to taking rowing exercises.  Left to right, Nedra Herz, Mary McKay, Flora Mae Prescott, Bettie Lou Slaughter, Virginia Constable, Marion Kline, Margaret Barnhisel, Viola Bunker, Virginia Putnam, Bettie Christy, Isobel Millier.
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

Handwritten on edge of negative: 1[?]

funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,” 28 Jul. 1940

Actors Linda Ware and Leo Carillo with the event announcer at the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration. A candid photo shows the announcer reading from a script while Ware looks over her shoulder in Carillo’s direction. She holds “A Partial List of Songs by Irving Berlin” in one hand. Carrillo faces left, his head turned toward the camera. A crowd of attendees stands in the background. Probably photographed at Santa Monica High School.

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

description_tesim: Photograph of Santa Monica Civic Opera singers portraying Rigoletto, Sparafucile and Count Monterone from the opera, “Rigoletto.” The singer portraying Rigoletto (possibly Charles Burton) wears an embroidered velvet doublet with sunburst collar, padded to reveal the character’s hunched back. He holds a bauble (a mock scepter) in one hand. To his left, Louis Statham portrays the hired assassin Sparafucile, wearing a Romani (Gypsy) style outfit with a loose shirt and pants, vest and waist sash. The outfit is completed with a scarf tied around his head and an earring in one ear. Laurin Malcolm, in costume as Count Monterone, wears an embellished tunic with ruff collar and cuffs, belt and stockings. They are in front of a set background painted to look like ornate paneled walls from the Duke’s palace. Photo possibly taken at John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica.

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: #10 to 20-. La Boheme. SM Civic Opera. June 1955. June Moss. B Ludwig.
Handwritten along edge of negative: 18
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: A notice for a production of La Bohème, performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 21 Aug. 1955: D6

Six opera singers performing in a production of Giacomo Puccini’s, La Bohème. The four men and two women perform in period costume. Singers portraying Colline and Marcello direct their attention to Musetta (portrayed by Barbara Ludwig), while singers portraying Schaunard and Rodolfo stand and kneel beside the character of Mimi (June Moss) sitting up in bed. The painted backdrop and set props recreate an attic apartment in Paris, while windows painted with snow-covered panes suggest the winter season outdoors. Props include a wood-burning stove, a table and chairs, a market basket, and a painting on the wall. An easel stands near the window. Microphones and an amplifier can be viewed at the stage’s edge, while a piano player and violinists are visible in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: A notice for a production of La Bohème, performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 21 Aug. 1955: D6

Four opera singers performing in a production of Giacomo Puccini’s, La Bohème. The men (wearing period costumes) portray Schaunard (a musician), Colline (a philosopher), Marcello (a painter) and Rodolfo (a writer).  They stand center stage surrounding a table set with candles and a bottle of wine. Schaunard stands on a chair. The painted backdrop and set props recreate their shared attic apartment, including a wood-burning stove, a market basket next to the table, a wall painting, and an easel propped near the window - painted here with snow-covered panes to suggest the winter season outdoors. Microphones and an amplifier can be viewed at the stage’s edge, while a piano player, cellist, and violinists are visible in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: A notice for a production of La Bohème, performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 21 Aug. 1955: D6

Six opera singers performing in a production of Giacomo Puccini’s, La Bohème. The four men and two women perform in period costume. June Moss, as Mimi, lays on her deathbed, the spotlight focused on her. In the background, singers portraying Marcello, Schaunard, Rodolfo, Colline, and Musetta (Barbara Ludwig) talk. The painted backdrop and set props recreate an attic apartment in Paris, while windows painted with snow-covered panes suggest the winter season outdoors. Microphones and an amplifier can be viewed at the stage’s edge, while the pianist and her music sheet are viewed in the foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera production of “Rigoletto.” The singers, portraying nobles and courtiers, wear costumes evoking 16th century attire - the female singers wear long gowns with full skirts and puffed sleeves. The men wear stockings, doublets with paneled sleeves, ruff collars, and baldrics (for holding a sword) across their chests. At left, performers portraying the Duke of Mantua and Rigoletto stand opposite two noblemen, possibly Count Monterone and Count Ceprano. The stage set represents a room in the Duke’s palace, replete with paneled walls set with Corinthian inspired columns and a frieze over a curtained passageway. Two family crests hang high above the painted scenery. At left sits a throne (an ornate wooden chair covered with a canopy and placed on an elevated platform); a chandelier hangs overhead at center. Musicians viewed from orchestra pit at foreground. Additionally, June Moss (second from right) performs as a noble in attendance.
member_of_collections_ssim: Adelbert Bartlett Papers, 1922-1950

description_tesim: Two actors from the Pacific Palisades Players theatre group, posed in character. Both are seated on a bench. The woman smiles at the camera while knitting fabric. A knitting bag rests on her lap. The man seated to the right directs his gaze toward her. Probably photographed in Pacific Palisades.

The Pacific Palisades Players were a community theatre group. They staged performances for local charity and community events and appeared often at the Ebsen Playhouse in Pacific Palisades. The group was sponsored by the Los Angeles Department of Recreation and Parks.

description_tesim: A notice for this production of "The Barber of Seville," performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at John Adams Junior High School, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 11 Dec. 1955: E5

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in "The Barber of Seville." The singers, wearing 18th century Spanish clothing, portray Count Almavida (dressed as a soldier), Bartolo, and Rosina (portrayed by Natalie Garrotto). The players are surrounded by a stage set recreating a room in Bartolo’s home. The set, painted with decorative paneled walls and a faux fireplace, is also decorated with end tables, chairs, a curtained window and candelabras. A pianist is visible from the orchestra pit. Performed at John Adams Junior High School, Santa Monica.
Image reversed.

description_tesim: Photograph shows a second floor campus building entrance, seen from the vantage point of a patio or rest area with benches and drinking fountains. Following the path from the covered entry, a sculptural work called “The Viking” stands between two sets of windows on the left. The pathway continues to an additional entry and, likely, to the adjacent building. The curved walkway follows the same lines as the first-floor windows, partially seen from this view. Photographed at Santa Monica High School.
Portions of Santa Monica High School were destroyed during the earthquake of 1933. Its reconstruction fell under the Works Project Administration (WPA). The project was completed in 1939.

description_tesim: Opera singers performing in Act 1 of Die Fledermaus. One male, wearing a tailcoat and tuxedo pants, portrays Gabriel von Einstein. He sings at center and is flanked two women - at right, a singer in the role of his wife, Rosalind; at left, her chambermaid, Adele.  The setting is of a home interior with tables, chairs, a settee and an ornate privacy partition. Potted plants create a backdrop for the open-air stage. Beyond the stage, two accompanying pianists can be viewed situated on a walkway separating the stage from a small reflecting pool pictured at middle ground, with audience members seen at foreground.  Performed August 27, 1965, during the 28th annual “Symphonies by the Sea” event, Open-Air Memorial Theater, Santa Monica High School Campus.
Symphonies by the Sea was a free-admission annual symphonic and operatic season which started at Santa Monica High School, then moved on to Santa Monica City College.
Event noted in a Los Angeles Times article, "Symphony-by-the-Sea Programs Scheduled," 11 July 1965: WS7..

description_tesim: Event noted in a Los Angeles Times article, "Symphony-by-the-Sea Programs Scheduled," 11 July 1965: WS7.

Dancers performing in Act 2 of the operetta, Die Fledermaus. Singers wearing late-19th century ball gowns and tuxedos watch and applaud as two pairs of dancers perform at center stage. Potted plants create a backdrop for the open-air stage, and separate the set from the girls’ gym building seen on the left. Set props include garden tables, benches, and banquettes, while a pair of lanterns can be seen flanking a set of steps in the background. Performed August 27, 1965, during the 28th annual “Symphonies by the Sea” event, Open-Air Memorial Theater, Santa Monica High School Campus.
Symphonies by the Sea was a free-admission annual symphonic and operatic season which started at Santa Monica High School, then moved on to Santa Monica City College.
